title: Inauguration

jokes.text: Mike stakes his reputation that Selina Meyer will 'never be vice president ever, ever again' — immediately before she becomes vice president again
jokes.setup: Reporter asks if Selina would consider becoming VP again. Mike emphatically denies it: 'I stake my reputation on this. Selina Meyer will never be vice president ever, ever again.'
jokes.punchline: The entire episode is about her becoming VP again — the viewer knows this dramatic irony is going to be paid off immediately.
jokes.explanation: Classic Veep cold-open dramatic irony. Mike's confidence makes the payoff richer. The triple 'ever, ever again' signals the show's self-awareness. Rewatch value is high because the audience knows what's coming. Character-specific because Mike's professional haplessness is his defining trait.

jokes.text: Mike's non-answer about Russia: 'The chances of this happening in Russia, nyet.'
jokes.setup: Mike is asked a pointed political question and deflects with a 'silver lining' observation.
jokes.punchline: 'Nyet' — the single Russian word dropped in as a punchline, delivering both the joke and the deflection simultaneously.
jokes.explanation: Economy is the standout here — the joke is fully delivered in one word. Mike's press secretary spin of a constitutional crisis into 'at least we're not Russia' is perfectly in-character. The 'nyet' as the lone punchline word is slick. Doesn't quite hit 8 because the Russia deflection joke is a familiar political comedy beat.

jokes.text: Selina tells Tom James 'You're gonna be an accidental president.' He replies: 'Pot, kettle.'
jokes.setup: Selina patronizingly frames Tom as an 'accidental president' while trying to negotiate her place in his administration.
jokes.punchline: Tom's two-word comeback ('Pot, kettle') deflates her authority instantly and reminds both her and the audience that she was also an accidental president.
jokes.explanation: Two words that do enormous work. The economy is exceptional — 'Pot, kettle' truncates a well-worn idiom and lets the silence do the rest. Character-specific: Tom's patrician cool allows him to dismantle Selina with minimal effort. The joke earns its score because it's built on the entire premise of the series, not a cheap gag.

jokes.text: Selina demands Secretary of State; Tom offers Vice President. 'That's literally the least you can do.' 'Vice president. Take it or leave it.'
jokes.setup: Selina wants Secretary of State as the minimum acceptable offer. Tom counters with VP.
jokes.punchline: She correctly identifies VP as 'literally the least you can do' — a job she already held and despised — while he calmly ignores her logic.
jokes.explanation: Solid negotiation comedy grounded in Selina's desperation and Tom's leverage. The line 'literally the least you can do' is well-observed — VP is constitutionally the minimum — but the joke relies on setup from the series more than exceptional writing in the moment.

jokes.text: 'I wouldn't be your veep if there were a grassy knoll full of Jodie Foster fans in the front row at your inauguration.'
jokes.setup: Tom keeps insisting she take the VP position. Selina needs to articulate how strongly she refuses.
jokes.punchline: She constructs an elaborate fantasy involving JFK assassination imagery (grassy knoll) AND a Jodie Foster reference (John Hinckley shooting Reagan) — wishing assassination on Tom while refusing the job.
jokes.explanation: Genuinely sharp dark comedy. Merging two separate presidential assassination references into a single hyperbolic refusal is original and specific. 'Jodie Foster fans' is the precise, unexpected detail that elevates this above a generic dark joke. Very quotable and distinctly Selina's unfiltered viciousness.

jokes.text: 'General George Washington could climb out of his grave right now and I would rather eat out his zombified wooden asshole twice a day than be his vice fucking... fucking anything!'
jokes.setup: Continuing her refusal of the VP position, Selina escalates her hyperbole.
jokes.punchline: The image of eating out zombie Washington's 'wooden asshole' (referencing the myth of wooden teeth) is so specific and grotesque it becomes transcendent; 'twice a day' adds the perfect escalating detail.
jokes.explanation: This is a candidate for one of the great Veep lines. The specificity is surgical: 'zombified' acknowledges the absurdity, 'wooden' ties to the mythology of Washington's wooden teeth (a historical misconception that becomes the perfect punchline detail), and 'twice a day' is the escalating kicker that signals genuine comic craft. Only Selina Meyer — entitled, hyperbolic, furiously eloquent in her vulgarity — delivers this. Justifiably scores near the top of the episode.

jokes.text: Tom responds to Selina's epic tirade with: 'Okay, let's put a pin in the VP for now.'
jokes.setup: Selina has just delivered the most operatic, obscene refusal imaginable.
jokes.punchline: Tom responds with total corporate banality, as if she's said nothing more alarming than 'I'd prefer the window seat.'
jokes.explanation: The joke is almost entirely structural: the contrast between the volcanic outburst and Tom's managerial calm. 'Let's put a pin in it' is a perfect comedic reset. Works specifically because Tom's unflappable quality is his defining character trait — only he could absorb this and respond with a meeting-room cliché. Earns a high score because the economy is near-perfect.

jokes.text: Kent reveals Selina was rated 43rd most effective president. 'Out of how many?' '44.' Right ahead of James Buchanan, 'who many feel caused the Civil War.'
jokes.setup: Kent says scholars rated her 43rd most effective president ever.
jokes.punchline: There have only been 44 presidents — she's second-to-last — and the only person below her is widely blamed for starting the Civil War.
jokes.explanation: Elegantly structured two-beat reveal: first the number ('43rd'), then the qualifier ('out of 44'), then the kicker (Buchanan/Civil War). Each beat lands before the next arrives. Slightly off-peak on originality because 'worst president' historical jokes are a familiar form, but the execution and timing are crisp.

jokes.text: 'Kent, can you give a girl some warning next time before you jam it in the back door?' / 'Data gives no warning, ma'am.'
jokes.setup: Selina reacts to Kent's brutal ranking data with a sexual innuendo complaint.
jokes.punchline: Kent's literal, data-worshipping response ('Data gives no warning') is both a deadpan rebuff AND philosophically true to his character.
jokes.explanation: The double-entendre setup is straightforward, but Kent's response is perfectly in character — he's so devoted to empiricism that he treats 'data' as an elemental force beyond social obligation. This could only be this exchange between these two specific characters.

jokes.text: Selina suggests a 'first White House lesbian wedding' as legacy. 'Oh, God. I can't take that much acoustic guitar.'
jokes.setup: Someone suggests Catherine and Marjorie's wedding as a legacy moment for Selina.
jokes.punchline: Selina's objection isn't principled — it's aesthetic: she can't endure the indie folk music that would accompany it.
jokes.explanation: The joke works on two levels: Selina's shallow self-absorption (a historic milestone is rejected for musical taste reasons) and the stereotype deployed with enough specificity ('acoustic guitar') to feel sharp rather than lazy. Her dismissal of her own daughter's relationship is darkly funny in Selina's signature way.

jokes.text: 'Well, then why don't we send warships out into the South China Seas?' / 'Sea.' / 'I want my Nobel Peace Prize!'
jokes.setup: Selina is told China has gone silent on Tibet. She immediately proposes military escalation.
jokes.punchline: The grammatical correction ('Sea') juxtaposed with her immediate pivot to wanting the Nobel Peace Prize reveals the absurdity: she's proposing war to win a peace prize.
jokes.explanation: Three-beat structure: reckless military proposal → pedantic grammar correction (absurdist) → 'I want my Nobel Peace Prize!' The Nobel line lands with force because it's so self-evidently contradictory and because it's delivered with genuine petulance. Strong character integration — this is the show's thesis about Selina in a single exchange.

jokes.text: Selina shouts 'Legacy! Legacy meeting adjourned.' as if screaming the word is itself the legacy plan.
jokes.setup: The staff meeting has devolved into a chaotic shouting of unfeasible legacy ideas.
jokes.punchline: Selina punctuates it by simply yelling 'Legacy!' twice as if volume equals accomplishment, then formally adjourning.
jokes.explanation: A solid character beat — Selina's manic, wishful thinking distilled into a single word shouted repeatedly. The formal 'meeting adjourned' after the outburst adds the bureaucratic contrast that Veep thrives on. Not exceptional but competently funny.

jokes.text: Dan muses about needing an agent to take the 'Dan Egan brand to the next level.' Ben: 'Too bad Goebbels killed himself.'
jokes.setup: Dan is self-aggrandizing about his brand and needing a top-tier agent.
jokes.punchline: Ben's casual suggestion of Goebbels — Nazi propaganda minister — as Dan's ideal agent is dark, historically specific, and devastating as a character read.
jokes.explanation: Economy is outstanding — one sentence does everything. Ben's exhausted misanthropy and Dan's narcissism are both perfectly captured. The Nazi reference is not gratuitous here; Goebbels is specifically the minister of propaganda and self-promotion, making it a precise insult rather than a shock-value name-drop. Ben's flat delivery of the line is essential to the joke landing.

jokes.text: 'The only president to pee sitting down since FDR. Is that gonna be my legacy, Ben?' / 'Well, I pee sitting down sometimes if it's gonna be a longy.'
jokes.setup: Selina laments that her presidential legacy may be reduced to a toilet habit.
jokes.punchline: Ben's earnest, casual confession that he also sits down for long pees completely deflates her self-pity with irrelevant oversharing.
jokes.explanation: 'A longy' is a perfect coinage — simple, vulgar, and unexpectedly delightful. Ben's character (world-weary, overly frank, unimpressed by anyone) makes this feel inevitable. The joke successfully deflates Selina's self-pity while also being genuinely funny as a standalone non-sequitur.

jokes.text: Ben: 'Well, I pee sitting down sometimes if it's gonna be a longy.'
jokes.setup: Selina is asking Ben to validate her legacy concerns about being the first female president to pee sitting down.
jokes.punchline: Ben matter-of-factly shares that he also pees sitting down for long sessions, utterly deflating the moment.
jokes.explanation: Ben's admission is perfectly timed and completely in character — his world-weary, no-filter honesty puncturing Selina's self-pity. 'A longy' is an inspired word choice that manages to be both gross and charming. The fact that he uses this as a casual equivalence to her historic presidency is the joke. Justifies 8 through economy, character specificity, and the brilliant anti-climax of the word 'longy.'

jokes.text: Selina tells staff to find Qataris at a Lamborghini dealership or 'wherever they have prostitutes.'
jokes.setup: Selina wants to use Qatari intermediaries to broker the Tibet deal.
jokes.punchline: Her casual instruction to find them via Lamborghinis or prostitutes plays on Gulf state wealth stereotypes, delivered with presidential matter-of-factness.
jokes.explanation: Funny because of Selina's breezy tone and the juxtaposition of diplomatic need with casual stereotype. But it leans on a familiar joke template (rich Gulf state = Lamborghinis/prostitutes) without adding a significant twist. Functional rather than exceptional.

jokes.text: 'Are there any pictures where the president isn't yelling at me?' / 'As far as I can tell, no such picture exists in the known universe.'
jokes.setup: Gary is overseeing the packing of Selina's photos with DC elites.
jokes.punchline: Every single photograph of Selina and Gary shows her yelling at him — not a single exception in the 'known universe.'
jokes.explanation: 'Known universe' is the comic escalation that elevates a straightforward character joke. The line captures the Selina/Gary dynamic with precision — her constant abuse of him is so consistent it's cosmologically documented. Character-specific and economical.

jokes.text: 'No, New Hampshire is just a fancy word for it's cold outside, so I don't shave my pubes.'
jokes.setup: Uncle Jeff sends resumes from New Hampshire. Gary/Selina dismisses them.
jokes.punchline: New Hampshire is redefined entirely as a climate-based grooming decision.
jokes.explanation: An unexpected geographic-to-grooming leap that works through sheer specificity and commitment. The phrase 'fancy word for' is doing structural work, making the non-sequitur feel like genuine logic. Slightly docked for character integration — could be delivered by several characters.

jokes.text: Selina tells the Chinese diplomat she'll be 'continuing on... as... um... as vice president.' The painful pause before admitting it.
jokes.setup: Selina is explaining her political future to the Chinese diplomat in order to keep the Tibet deal alive.
jokes.punchline: She cannot make herself say 'vice president' — the word physically resists being spoken — and the long ellipsis before it is the joke.
jokes.explanation: A payoff on the cold-open joke. The humor is entirely in the performance and structure — the pauses, the ellipses, the 'as, um... as' — signaling that Selina's pride is being visibly murdered in real time. Earns high character integration because only Selina's specific brand of humiliated grandiosity makes this land.

jokes.text: 'It's really more of a copresident position. Wouldn't you agree with that, Ben?' / 'Sure.'
jokes.setup: Selina has just told the Chinese diplomat she'll be VP, then immediately tries to soften the blow.
jokes.punchline: She invents the term 'copresident' and desperately looks to Ben for validation. His one-word, utterly flat 'Sure' communicates total disbelief while technically providing the support she asked for.
jokes.explanation: Ben's 'Sure' is a masterpiece of non-commitment. The joke is in the gap between what she needs and what he gives her. Economy is near-perfect — one syllable carries the entire weight of Ben's exhausted, cynical loyalty.

jokes.text: 'Tom James could be healthier, I have to say... well, I suspect this will be of great interest to the Chinese.'
jokes.setup: Selina is hinting that Tom James may not survive his presidency, thereby making her VP position potentially very powerful.
jokes.punchline: The Chinese diplomat immediately grasps the implication — that Selina might soon be president — and responds with enthusiasm.
jokes.explanation: Darkly funny geopolitical implication comedy — Selina is essentially hinting at the president's death to a foreign government to advance her own interests. The diplomat's quick uptake ('of great interest') plays it completely straight, which is funnier than any overt reaction.

jokes.text: 'Oh, right, no touchy the ladies. Oh, no, no. It's fine. It's private. Just don't let the lunatics back home know.'
jokes.setup: The Chinese diplomat appears to go for a handshake or physical greeting with Selina.
jokes.punchline: Selina's self-deprecating joke about managing her own cultural optics — 'don't let the lunatics back home know' — acknowledges the absurdity of American political puritanism while being breezy about it.
jokes.explanation: A functional joke about the performative nature of political image management. 'Lunatics back home' is a good phrase. Not special, but lands.

jokes.text: Tom: 'You have decided to throw away every last shred of dignity you have and accept my offer of the vice presidency.' Selina: 'I... I guess so, yeah.'
jokes.setup: Selina arrives to accept the VP job after her violent refusal.
jokes.punchline: Tom names what's happening ('throw away every last shred of dignity') and she can only confirm it with a defeated, halting 'I... I guess so, yeah.'
jokes.explanation: Tom making explicit what the audience is already feeling — this IS a dignity death — and Selina's collapsed confirmation is genuinely moving and funny simultaneously. The comedy is earned through cumulative series context. High marks for structure and character integration.

jokes.text: 'You're gonna be a partner... and a very important part of my administration.' / 'You're a motherfucker.' / 'No, I'm serious.'
jokes.setup: Tom is being (falsely) warm and reassuring about the VP role.
jokes.punchline: Selina's blunt 'You're a motherfucker' is her genuine assessment of his condescension, and his response 'No, I'm serious' treats it as a misunderstanding rather than an insult.
jokes.explanation: Tom's refusal to acknowledge the insult as an insult — treating it as simple skepticism — is the comedic engine. It simultaneously shows his unflappable patrician confidence and her impotent rage. Well-structured exchange.

jokes.text: 'It's like a shitty Groundhog Day.'
jokes.setup: Selina is being told she'll be 'at every meeting' as VP — the same promises made before her first VP term.
jokes.punchline: She recognizes the repetition explicitly: 'Groundhog Day' captures both the narrative loop and her trapped feeling. 'Shitty' is the key modifier.
jokes.explanation: A self-aware pop culture reference that works because it's accurate and Selina-specific. The modifier 'shitty' is doing important work — it's not just Groundhog Day, it's the bad version. Economy is exceptional. Slightly docked for originality since the Groundhog Day comparison is not novel.

jokes.text: Richard introduces 'hot interns' Colt, Brady, and Mason — three conventionally attractive men — to Dan, who wanted hot female interns.
jokes.setup: Dan told Richard to get 'hot interns.' Richard delivers: three conventionally attractive college-age men.
jokes.punchline: Richard genuinely did not understand 'hot interns' meant women. He defends his choices: 'by any objective standards, Colt, Brady, and Mason are unequivocally hot, not not.'
jokes.explanation: Richard's literal-minded obliviousness is perfectly in character — he applied objective beauty standards without reading the implied sexist subtext. 'Unequivocally hot, not not' is a Richard-ism that's genuinely funny in its phrasing. Good character integration.

jokes.text: Colt (male intern) brings Dan an exceptional latte. 'Oh, my God, this coffee is exceptional.' / 'Can we please keep him?' / 'Yeah, absolutely.'
jokes.setup: Dan is frustrated about getting male interns instead of female ones.
jokes.punchline: One of the male interns immediately wins Dan over with a perfect latte — his sexist agenda crumbles at the first taste of good coffee.
jokes.explanation: The quick pivot from outrage to delight is funny in its smallness — Dan's principles vanish instantly for a good latte. Captures his transactional nature efficiently.

jokes.text: 'I want you guys to get used to two things... killing it daily on the Hill and nights rated PG-squirteen.' / 'Baller.' / 'Baller.'
jokes.setup: Jonah is welcoming his new interns with a speech about life in his office.
jokes.punchline: 'PG-squirteen' is a nonsense rating that Jonah invents and delivers with total confidence. The interns immediately validate it with synchronized 'Baller.'
jokes.explanation: 'PG-squirteen' is a perfectly Jonah invention — trying to sound cool and edgy, landing on something that means nothing. The synchronized 'Baller' response is the payoff: his subordinates immediately reinforce his delusion. Character-specific and oddly quotable.

jokes.text: 'Statesman-ish, which is a joke, of course.'
jokes.setup: The Chinese interpreter tells Selina that President Lu is pleased to work with Senator James, 'quite the statesman.'
jokes.punchline: Selina mutters 'Statesman-ish, which is a joke, of course' — dismissing Tom while trying to maintain diplomatic decorum.
jokes.explanation: A quick, deft aside — Selina's petty jealousy of Tom slipping through her diplomatic mask. The '-ish' suffix and the awkward 'which is a joke, of course' self-correction are both nicely done.

jokes.text: 'Come on, you page-diddling Hawaiian fat fuck.'
jokes.setup: Tom needs a senator named Yinui to vote for him to avoid a tie.
jokes.punchline: His sotto voce stream of abuse — 'page-diddling Hawaiian fat fuck' — is the private unfiltered version of the man who presents as a statesman.
jokes.explanation: Part of Veep's signature move — revealing the vile private thoughts of publicly polished figures. 'Page-diddling' adds a corruption layer. But the insult relies partly on crude physical and ethnic mockery, docking earned_vs_cheap somewhat. Effective in context, not exceptional craft.

jokes.text: 'Thank you, Summerlin, you senile old piss sponge.'
jokes.setup: Senator Summerlin votes for Tom, helping his count.
jokes.punchline: Tom's simultaneous public gratitude ('Thank you, Summerlin') and private savage insult ('you senile old piss sponge') captures the entire show's thesis about political performance vs. reality.
jokes.explanation: 'Piss sponge' is an inspired compound insult — specific, organic, and degrading in a way that feels like it came from genuine contempt rather than a joke room thesaurus. The grammatical structure (gratitude + insult as single breath) is the comedy mechanism.

jokes.text: Someone explains that a Senate tie 'is broken by the president of the Senate. Or what we humans call the vice president.'
jokes.setup: The vote is tied and people are confused about the tiebreaker procedure.
jokes.punchline: 'Or what we humans call the vice president' — the condescension implied by 'we humans' is funny, suggesting the person being informed is somehow non-human in their ignorance.
jokes.explanation: 'We humans' is the funny phrase — bureaucratic condescension at a moment of total political chaos. A small but well-constructed line.

jokes.text: 'Secretary of state.' / 'God, I hate this country.' / 'I know.'
jokes.setup: Selina learns Doyle gets the Secretary of State job she wanted.
jokes.punchline: Her cosmic contempt — 'God, I hate this country' — and Ben's simple 'I know' is the perfect expression of their shared exhausted cynicism.
jokes.explanation: Six words between two characters that land like a eulogy for American political idealism. The economy is exceptional — 'God, I hate this country' / 'I know' — no elaboration, no inflation. Ben's 'I know' is the comedy: immediate, flat, total agreement. Highly quotable and emotionally resonant.

jokes.text: Gary's unhinged rant at Selina's staff after the loss: 'You fuckers. How dare you? That magnificent woman counted on you...' — someone calls him back to tell someone what's happening: 'Yeah, I got to call you back. Something amazing is happening.'
jokes.setup: Gary erupts at the assembled staff after Selina loses. His loyalty is total and unhinged.
jokes.punchline: Someone in the background calls someone else to report Gary's meltdown as 'something amazing' — treating his loyalty explosion as entertainment.
jokes.explanation: Gary's rant is both funny and unexpectedly touching — his love for Selina is the most genuine thing in the show, and it's expressed in the most embarrassing way possible. The background call ('something amazing is happening') adds perfect comic distance. High character integration because this is Gary's defining moment in the series.

jokes.text: 'Oh, shut up!' — Gary turns on the staff. 'You screwed her the worst. In all the ways.'
jokes.setup: Gary's rant turns specifically accusatory at one staff member.
jokes.punchline: 'In all the ways' — the specificity of 'all the ways' without elaborating what the ways are is the joke. It's both an accusation and a mystery.
jokes.explanation: A small but effective line within the Gary rant sequence. The vague totality of 'in all the ways' is both funny and vaguely menacing. Part of the escalating character comedy of Gary's breakdown.

jokes.text: Gary: 'I know you make fun of me and I know that you think that I'm funny and I'm funny, ha, ha, ha. Well, at least I cared. I did my job! I fucking cared!'
jokes.setup: Gary acknowledges that everyone laughs at him.
jokes.punchline: His defense isn't dignity — it's that his degrading devotion was genuine. The joke pivots into something almost moving, which makes it funnier and more uncomfortable.
jokes.explanation: This is where Gary's character reaches its apex. The self-awareness ('I know you think I'm funny') combined with the defiant pride ('I fucking cared!') is genuinely complex comedy writing. It works as a joke, as character revelation, and as pathos simultaneously. Very high character integration — this is Gary Walsh's thesis statement. The 'ha, ha, ha' mimicry of others' mockery is a particularly sharp touch.

jokes.text: 'Well, that just kind of made this whole year worth it.' / 'Yeah.'
jokes.setup: Gary has just had his emotional breakdown-rant.
jokes.punchline: Dan and Amy's immediate deadpan assessment — Gary's anguish was the highlight of their year — is brutally funny.
jokes.explanation: Perfect punctuation to Gary's breakdown. The callousness of Dan and Amy — treating Gary's genuine anguish as entertainment — is the show's signature move. Economy is exceptional: 'this whole year worth it' and 'Yeah' says everything.

jokes.text: Ben's young-adult fiction pitch: 'Mine takes place in an alternate timeline where overweight, alcoholic ex-chiefs of staff are considered a sexual prize.'
jokes.setup: Someone has come to pitch a young adult fiction idea. Ben is in the hallway.
jokes.punchline: Ben has apparently been developing a self-insert fantasy novel where men like himself are sexually desirable — delivered with complete deadpan earnestness.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines. The specificity of 'overweight, alcoholic ex-chiefs of staff' is doing enormous work — it's Ben's exact situation, delivered without self-pity but with unmistakable wishful thinking. The deadpan framing as a 'young adult fiction' pitch makes it even more absurd. High originality — this joke could not exist in any other show, and probably only Ben would say it.

jokes.text: Kent: 'Alternate timelines are a crutch for lazy sci-fi premises, e.g., "Star Trek" with the exception of "Deep Space Nine."'
jokes.setup: Ben pitches his alternate-timeline novel. Kent critiques it.
jokes.punchline: Kent's objection is entirely genre-craft based ('lazy sci-fi premise') with a completely sincere DS9 exemption — revealing a devoted Trekkie under the data-analyst exterior.
jokes.explanation: Exceptional character joke. Kent's robotic precision and data-worship applied to science fiction fandom is a perfect comic revelation. The DS9 carve-out is both a specific fandom opinion AND funny because it's the 'serious' Trek — of course Kent likes it. High originality because the joke works on multiple levels: character comedy, fandom comedy, and the absurdity of this being his critique of Ben's sad fantasy novel.

jokes.text: News anchor announces Montez will nominate 'Wall Street banker Charlie Baird... President Meyer dated the banker briefly late last year.'
jokes.setup: The incoming president is naming her secretary of treasury.
jokes.punchline: It's Selina's ex. The anchor reports it with total neutrality, as if it's normal news — making the absurdity land harder.
jokes.explanation: A solid background joke that functions as both a character humiliation for Selina and a neat world-building detail. The news anchor's flat delivery makes it funnier than if characters reacted to it directly.

jokes.text: 'I should have relied on you more, Richard... if I had had 100 Richards, who knows what I could have done?'
jokes.setup: Selina is having a late-night, slightly drunk moment of reflection with Richard in the emptying White House.
jokes.punchline: She praises Richard with genuine warmth — but '100 Richards' is absurd enough to be funny, and her sincerity here is darkly comedic given how she treats people.
jokes.explanation: Gentle character comedy — Selina's rare genuine warmth and its slightly absurd expression. The '100 Richards' image is funny but the joke is more poignant than sharp.

jokes.text: Richard: 'Or it could turn out like one of those "Twilight Zone" scenarios where we all murder each other. I don't even remember how to drive.'
jokes.setup: Selina says 100 Richards could have achieved great things.
jokes.punchline: Richard's contribution to the hypothetical: they'd probably murder each other. Then, as a non-sequitur coda: 'I don't even remember how to drive.'
jokes.explanation: Richard's non-sequitur tangents are his comedic signature, and this is a well-executed example. The Twilight Zone reference is funny, but 'I don't even remember how to drive' is funnier — a tiny, mundane confession that lands because it's completely irrelevant to the conversation and completely sincere.

jokes.text: Selina: 'I need a wallet. And stamps. I've got to get stamps.'
jokes.setup: Selina is contemplating life after the presidency for the first time.
jokes.punchline: The most powerful person in the world realizing she doesn't know how to do basic adult tasks — and starting with stamps — is both sad and funny.
jokes.explanation: The humor is in the scale collapse — from 'President of the United States' to 'I need stamps.' Richard's 'Yeah' in agreement makes it more melancholy. The comedy of powerful people stripped of their support structure is a classic Veep theme, and this is one of the cleaner expressions of it.

jokes.text: 'If I was a little girl and you said to me, "What do you want to do?" I would have said, "Please, can I be president?" And then it turned out to be the 12 loneliest months of my life.'
jokes.setup: Selina is having a rare unguarded moment of reflection with Richard.
jokes.punchline: The childhood dream ('Please, can I be president?') versus the reality ('12 loneliest months of my life') is the joke — and it's genuinely dark.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's more emotionally resonant jokes — funny because it's true, sad because it's funny. 'Please, can I be president?' with that childlike 'please' is a perfect touch. The comedy and the tragedy are inseparable here.

jokes.text: Richard's rambling revelation: 'Maybe my auntie was actually my real mother and my mother was actually my grandmother. Ah, wow, that's starting to make a whole lot of sense. Everybody knew about it but me, too.'
jokes.setup: Richard is responding to Selina's mention of loneliness being like his aunt's experience.
jokes.punchline: He goes down a rabbit hole about his family structure and comes to a real-time epiphany about his parentage — completely inappropriate to the moment, delivered with total earnestness.
jokes.explanation: Richard's non-sequitur tangent reaching a genuinely unexpected family-secret revelation is peak Richard comedy. The real-time processing ('Ah, wow, that's starting to make a whole lot of sense') is the funniest beat — he's having a breakthrough in the middle of the President of the United States's breakdown.

jokes.text: 'Are we praying, ma'am?' / 'No, I'm just gonna lie down.'
jokes.setup: Selina is overcome and apparently sinking to the floor or bowing her head.
jokes.punchline: Richard misreads the gesture as prayer; Selina clarifies she's just lying down — which is somehow more pathetic.
jokes.explanation: The exchange works through perfect economy and the gentle deflation — prayer would be dignified; lying down on the White House floor is not. Richard's genuine confusion and Selina's matter-of-fact response are both perfectly timed.

jokes.text: 'Oh, my God, I hope I didn't fuck Richard.'
jokes.setup: Selina wakes up disoriented the morning of the inauguration.
jokes.punchline: Her first coherent thought is whether she slept with Richard — her loyal, sweet, socially oblivious aide.
jokes.explanation: The callback to the previous scene (Richard staying with her) makes this land harder. The specificity of Richard — not just any aide, but the gentle, guileless one — makes it funnier than a generic 'I hope I didn't sleep with someone' joke. Economy is excellent.

jokes.text: Selina wakes up confused after apparently sleeping next to Richard. 'Oh, God, I hope I didn't fuck Richard.'
jokes.setup: Selina fell asleep after asking Richard to stay. She wakes up and doesn't know what happened.
jokes.punchline: Her first conscious fear is that she had sex with Richard — which is both a commentary on her loneliness and a genuinely shocking joke.
jokes.explanation: An extraordinary morning-after joke. The horror in her voice, the specificity of Richard as the person she might have slept with, and the context (last night of her presidency, she was emotionally devastated, asked him to stay) makes this both hysterically funny and darkly sad. The joke crystallizes Selina's entire character state — desperate, powerful, alone, and now possibly having made the worst decision of her life. Justifies 8+ through the shock of the line, the context richness, perfect character integration, and the economy of getting all that in six words.

jokes.text: 'They can take away your presidency, they can take away your power, they can take away your dignity, but what is the one thing they cannot take away from you?' / 'My beauty.' / 'That's right. Let's go.'
jokes.setup: Gary is giving Selina a pep talk before greeting the incoming president.
jokes.punchline: He builds to a classic 'they can't take X' speech and the answer is 'my beauty' — Gary's worldview is entirely built around Selina's physical appearance, and she has fully internalized it.
jokes.explanation: The speech structure is excellent — the list of losses is genuinely somber, making the 'beauty' punchline land with maximum contrast. It also perfectly captures Gary's entire character: he is Selina's beauty coach, hype man, and emotional support in one delusional package. Both characters are perfectly captured in six words.

jokes.text: 'Do you realize the whole goddamn Rayburn House Office Building can hear you and your twink army in here cock scraping each other's esophagi?'
jokes.setup: Jonah and his new male interns are making noise playing basketball or roughhousing.
jokes.punchline: 'Twink army' and 'cock scraping each other's esophagi' is an escalating compound image that's both vulgar and surprisingly specific.
jokes.explanation: Veep at its most aggressively vulgar. The compound insult construction is characteristic of the show. 'Esophagi' as the plural is a small precision detail that elevates the grossness into something almost scholarly. More shock value than craft, but effective.

jokes.text: 'They must be substantially less attractive than the congressman.' / 'And I almost hired an Iraqi war veteran. Third degree burns on half his face, but the guy had personality, so...'
jokes.setup: The congressman is explaining his rule: staff must be less attractive than him.
jokes.punchline: He almost hired a severely burned war veteran as a deliberate attractiveness buffer — the veteran's patriotic sacrifice is being used as a hiring strategy.
jokes.explanation: Dark but perfectly calibrated — the joke is about the congressman's vanity and his willingness to exploit a veteran's injury for image management. The 'but the guy had personality, so...' trailing off implies he almost didn't hire him because of that. Sharp social satire.

jokes.text: 'Because I'm repulsive both in appearance and manner.'
jokes.setup: The congressman asks Will to tell Jonah why he (Will) was hired.
jokes.punchline: Will's answer — 'Because I'm repulsive both in appearance and manner' — is a trained, rehearsed self-deprecation delivered without emotion.
jokes.explanation: A perfect line — the economy is exceptional ('repulsive both in appearance and manner' covers everything with clinical precision) and the fact that this is clearly a rehearsed, institutional answer makes it funnier. Will has had to internalize this self-assessment as a job qualification. High quotability.

jokes.text: 'Now let me give you some advice before you're yanked out of here like a cheerleader's unwanted fetus.'
jokes.setup: The congressman is giving Jonah unsolicited advice.
jokes.punchline: The simile — comparing Jonah's imminent removal to an aborted fetus — is jarring, specific, and darkly funny in its disproportionate vulgarity.
jokes.explanation: Veep's signature of choosing the most extreme possible simile — the cheerleader fetus comparison is classically the show's style. More shock value than genuine craft, but the absurd specificity ('cheerleader's unwanted') adds some texture.

jokes.text: 'You look like Clark Kent if they dug up Christopher Reeve's corpse to play the part.'
jokes.setup: The congressman tells Jonah's intern to take off his glasses.
jokes.punchline: The insult: he looks like Clark Kent, but the corpse of Christopher Reeve's version — too similar and too dead.
jokes.explanation: The specificity of Christopher Reeve (not just 'Superman') and the corpse detail makes this a well-constructed insult. It's not just that he looks like Superman — it's the DEAD version of the most famous Superman actor. Multiple layers in a short construction. High quotability.

jokes.text: Jonah celebrates after leaving: 'Fuck that guy.' / 'Come on, let's go. Game on. Here we go. All right, ready? Alley-oop!' / 'My fucking nuts!'
jokes.setup: Jonah leaves the congressman's office and immediately tries to be cool with his intern.
jokes.punchline: The alley-oop basketball play immediately results in Jonah being hit in the testicles.
jokes.explanation: A classic Jonah physical humiliation that works because it's fast and because his excitement ('Game on!') immediately precedes his injury. Not exceptional craft but solid execution of Jonah's reliable comic function.

jokes.text: Amy to Ben's new colleague: 'You've interviewed here so many times, I'd think that you'd know the place like the back of your hand by now.'
jokes.setup: Candi is being shown around the White House as the incoming chief of staff.
jokes.punchline: Amy's line implies Candi has been rejected from this job multiple times — a passive-aggressive congratulations that's actually an insult.
jokes.explanation: A well-constructed passive-aggressive insult — the surface meaning (you know the place well) is pleasant; the implication (you've failed here repeatedly) is cutting. Amy's character (bitter, sharp-tongued) makes this feel authentic.

jokes.text: Mike introducing his surrogate, twins, and three-year-old: 'Mike, she has the head circumference of a six-year-old.' / 'I'm six.'
jokes.setup: Mike's adopted daughter Ellen is introduced. Kent notes she has the head circumference of a six-year-old.
jokes.punchline: Ellen says 'I'm six' — meaning Mike has been lying about her age, or is confused about his own child's age.
jokes.explanation: A perfectly constructed two-beat joke. Kent's data-precise observation (head circumference) sets up the punchline, and Ellen's flat 'I'm six' delivers the information in the most efficient possible way. The joke works on multiple levels: Kent's ridiculous precision, Mike's apparent deception/confusion about his own child's age, and the child's guileless correction. Near-perfect structure and economy.

jokes.text: 'I think it was a slave state, so it might have been free.'
jokes.setup: Small talk about how much it cost to paint the Red Room 'back then.'
jokes.punchline: Someone notes that if it was a slave state, the labor would have been free — a casually devastating historical observation dropped into polite small talk.
jokes.explanation: A dark observational joke that lands because of the casual, polite-chat context in which it's deployed. The humor is in the dissonance. Slightly docked for character integration (unclear who delivers it) and earned_vs_cheap (the darkness is effective but the joke's structure is simple).

jokes.text: Small talk between Selina and Montez's family: 'Girls are the worst.' / 'Well, I also heard it might go down into the teens tonight.' (desperate pivot)
jokes.setup: Awkward pre-inauguration coffee with the incoming first family. Someone says 'girls are the worst.'
jokes.punchline: The conversation immediately pivots to weather — the most desperate possible escape from an awkward statement — in a sequence of failed small talk.
jokes.explanation: The awkward small talk sequence captures the excruciating politeness required when enemies must share coffee. The weather pivot after 'girls are the worst' is a recognizable social maneuver. Functional cringe comedy.

jokes.text: Selina: 'And where exactly in Ohio are you from, Laura?' / Montez: 'I grew up right outside of Cleveland, but after 15 years, I feel like I am 100% New Mexican.' / 'New Mexican, but not Mexican?'
jokes.setup: Small talk at the pre-inauguration coffee. Selina asks the new president, a Latina woman, where she's from.
jokes.punchline: When Montez clarifies she's 'New Mexican,' Selina's follow-up — 'New Mexican, but not Mexican?' — implies she's questioning Montez's ethnic identity. It's a passive-aggressive jab wrapped in geographic confusion.
jokes.explanation: A layered joke: on the surface it plays like a genuine geographic misunderstanding, but given the context (Selina despises Montez, who is Latina and has just beaten her), it reads as pointed. 'New Mexican, but not Mexican?' is both plausibly oblivious and potentially vicious — the ambiguity is the craft. High character integration: only Selina can make this read as both accident and weapon.

jokes.text: 'New Mexican, but not Mexican?' — Selina's confused response when Montez says she's '100% New Mexican.'
jokes.setup: Montez says she's been in New Mexico 15 years and feels '100% New Mexican.'
jokes.punchline: Selina's confusion between 'New Mexican' (state resident) and 'Mexican' (nationality) is either genuine or deliberate — 'New Mexican, but not Mexican?'
jokes.explanation: The follow-up to the Ohio confusion is this moment of Selina processing 'New Mexican' as a distinction she's apparently not familiar with. The question is devastating — is she genuinely this ignorant, or is she deliberately puncturing Montez? The ambiguity is the comedy. It caps the entire awkward exchange perfectly. Justifies 8+ through the callback structure, economy, and the exquisite cringe.

jokes.text: News anchor: 'President Meyer has taken the stage. And she's greeting a very attractive stranger.' / 'That is first daughter Catherine Meyer.'
jokes.setup: The inauguration ceremony begins. A news anchor comments on Selina greeting someone.
jokes.punchline: The anchor doesn't recognize Catherine as Selina's daughter — calling her 'a very attractive stranger' — implying either Catherine has changed dramatically or that Selina is so rarely photographed with her daughter that no one knows who she is.
jokes.explanation: A brilliant background joke. 'A very attractive stranger' is the anchor's professional, neutral phrasing — and then the reveal deflates it by showing how alienated Selina is from her own daughter. The anchor's follow-up 'Oh, wow. Too bad' (after learning it's her daughter) adds a devastating coda about Catherine's press image. Sharp, economical, and layered.

jokes.text: Selina to Doyle at the inauguration: 'Why'd you do it, Andrew? I offered you secretary of state.' / Doyle: 'You offered it to everybody in town.'
jokes.setup: Selina confronts Doyle about betraying her to get Montez elected.
jokes.punchline: Doyle's response — 'You offered it to everybody in town' — reveals that Selina's offer was worthless because she'd made it to everyone, making her political currency meaningless.
jokes.explanation: Doyle's line works as a perfect callback and a character revelation — Selina's desperation has made her offers valueless. The economy is excellent. The joke lands harder because we've seen her use the SecState offer all episode.

jokes.text: 'Your head is so far up Montez's ass. Next time it's Alejandro's birthday, he's gonna come all over your face.'
jokes.setup: Selina is furious with Doyle at the inauguration ceremony.
jokes.punchline: The graphic sexual threat about Montez's husband Alejandro is delivered as a political insult at a public ceremony, while the news anchor narrates their 'very special relationship' in the background.
jokes.explanation: The juxtaposition of the anchor's warm 'very special relationship' commentary playing over Selina's obscene threat is what makes this exceptional. The joke works on two levels simultaneously. The specific ethnic name 'Alejandro' adds to the vividness.

jokes.text: News anchor narrating: 'President Meyer taking a moment to catch up with her vice president and longtime political ally Andrew Doyle. Those two have a very special relationship.'
jokes.setup: Selina is visibly raging at Doyle at the public inauguration while the news cameras cover it.
jokes.punchline: The anchor's bland, professional interpretation of what is clearly a furious confrontation — 'very special relationship' — is a perfect ironic gloss.
jokes.explanation: The gap between what the anchor says ('very special relationship') and what we know is happening (explicit threats) is the classic irony engine. The anchor's professional blandness makes it land.

jokes.text: Tom James on Montez's speech: 'I had no idea her tits were that big.'
jokes.setup: Someone asks Tom what he thought of Montez's inaugural speech.
jokes.punchline: His only observation about the historic first woman becoming president is about her body — the patrician statesman unmasked as a boor.
jokes.explanation: Tom's mask-off moment — the 'statesman' can only see the incoming president's body, which is both a character revelation and a comment on political culture. Economy is excellent. The question was about the speech; his answer is entirely non-responsive.

jokes.text: 'Well, I've always dreamed of living in a small town, having a little place on Main Street, hanging a shingle out front, and running my own little multibillion-dollar hedge fund. Back to basics.'
jokes.setup: Someone asks Tom what's next for him.
jokes.punchline: He frames a 'back to basics, small-town life' dream and then immediately reveals the 'basics' are a multibillion-dollar hedge fund. The homespun language is a put-on.
jokes.explanation: A well-constructed ironic monologue that captures the entire class performance of elite American politicians — the folksiness is deliberate and hollow. The slow build through genuinely homespun images ('shingle,' 'Main Street') before the reveal ('multibillion-dollar hedge fund') is structurally excellent. 'Back to basics' as the kicker is perfect. High quotability.

jokes.text: Staff member compliments Selina on her 'nice shoes.' Selina: 'Oh, come on, man.'
jokes.setup: Selina is leaving the White House as a private citizen for the first time.
jokes.punchline: The best a staff member can offer as a goodbye is a compliment on her shoes — possibly because they have nothing else to say. Selina's 'Oh, come on, man' is her accepting that this is what it's come to.
jokes.explanation: A small but well-observed moment of deflation. The shoes compliment is the best the moment can offer — and it's pathetic. Selina's resigned response is perfect.

jokes.text: Selina: 'Well, I'm not good with good-byes.' / Ben: 'Or winning presidential elections.' / Selina: 'Well, that's not true.' / Ben: 'Yeah, it is.' / Selina: 'Okay.'
jokes.setup: Selina is making a joke about not being good with goodbyes.
jokes.punchline: Ben's addition — 'Or winning presidential elections' — is devastating in its accuracy. She tries to deny it; he flatly confirms it; she accepts it.
jokes.explanation: A perfectly structured five-beat exchange. The rhythm of her denial and his correction is impeccable. 'Yeah, it is.' and then Selina's quiet 'Okay' is the comedy — she just accepts it. Economy is exceptional. Ben's role as the truth-teller is at its most concentrated. One of the episode's best moments.

jokes.text: Selina's goodbye speech to staff: 'Um... I don't know what to say.' Gary: 'Was I supposed to write something, ma'am? Nobody told me.'
jokes.setup: Selina is saying goodbye to her staff. She's clearly expecting Gary to have prepared remarks.
jokes.punchline: Gary had no idea he was supposed to write a speech for this moment — underlining both Gary's incompetence and the chaos of the Meyer administration.
jokes.explanation: Gary's apology for not writing the speech nobody asked him to write — for a moment he didn't know was happening — encapsulates the entire Meyer administration. His confused helpfulness is perfect character comedy.

jokes.text: Mike: 'Uh, well, we gave America everything we had.' / Selina: 'Yeah, sure did.' / Mike: 'Inspiring words, ma'am. Godspeed.'
jokes.setup: Mike is making a farewell speech.
jokes.punchline: He calls his own empty platitude 'inspiring words' — completely unironic self-congratulation for saying nothing.
jokes.explanation: Mike calling his own hollow goodbye 'inspiring words' is perfectly in character — he's the show's exemplar of political spin turned inward. The self-congratulation for vacuous speech is pure Mike McLintock.

jokes.text: 'Gosh, from a distance, it looks really beautiful.' / 'Yeah, from a distance.'
jokes.setup: Looking back at the inauguration/White House from outside.
jokes.punchline: 'Yeah, from a distance' — Selina's repetition carries all the weight of her failed presidency: it only looked good from far away.
jokes.explanation: Deceptively simple. The repetition of 'from a distance' does all the work — it's Selina's entire presidency in four words. The echo structure (A says it admiringly, Selina repeats it bleakly) is elegant and economical. One of the more literary jokes in the episode.

jokes.text: 'Can I borrow somebody's phone? I have to check on Buddy.' / 'Oh, did you get a dog?'
jokes.setup: Amy mentions needing to check on Buddy.
jokes.punchline: Someone assumes Buddy is a dog — the mundane inference — and Amy is apparently asking after something or someone else entirely (possibly a boyfriend, a child, a pet iguana — the ambiguity is part of the joke).
jokes.explanation: A light misdirection joke that mostly functions as setup for a payoff the transcript doesn't resolve. Works as a small beat, not exceptional.

jokes.text: Plane engine fails immediately after takeoff. 'Small problem with one of the engines, Madam President. We're gonna have to take her back down.'
jokes.setup: Selina finally boards her plane to leave the White House and begin post-presidential life.
jokes.punchline: The plane immediately has an engine problem — the universe will not let her leave cleanly. Even her exit is a failure.
jokes.explanation: Classic Veep escalating indignity — even the exit is botched. The comedy is situational: just when she's finally going, she can't. The pilot's professional understatement ('small problem') while potentially describing a catastrophic failure is well-played.

jokes.text: 'Hell of a time to run out of gas.'
jokes.setup: The presidential plane has an engine problem.
jokes.punchline: Ben reduces a potential aviation emergency to 'run out of gas' — the most mundane possible framing for a presidential air emergency.
jokes.explanation: Ben's signature move — taking a potentially serious situation and deflating it to the most pedestrian possible description. Economy is exceptional. 'Hell of a time' is the right register of his exhausted affect.

jokes.text: After the plane fails and they must walk back, Selina: 'What am I walking on?' Crew: 'Grass, ma'am.'
jokes.setup: Selina has been living in the bubble of power so long she doesn't recognize what she's standing on.
jokes.punchline: 'Grass, ma'am.' — she has to be told what grass is. The comment is straight-faced, which makes it funnier.
jokes.explanation: A single-sentence joke that says everything about power and its insulation. The ex-president of the United States needs to be told she is standing on grass. The crew member's matter-of-fact 'Grass, ma'am' — treating this as a reasonable answer to a reasonable question — is perfect. One of the episode's great small jokes. Justifies 8+ through the total character integration (this is exactly who Selina has become), near-perfect economy, and the symbolic weight of the moment.

jokes.text: 'If you could just step away from the aircraft for a bit, we'll call you a motorcade.' / 'Oh, my God. What am I walking on?' / 'Grass, ma'am.'
jokes.setup: Selina is deposed from her failed plane escape and has to walk on the airfield.
jokes.punchline: The former president doesn't know she's standing on grass — she has been so insulated from ordinary surfaces that walking on a lawn is disorienting.
jokes.explanation: A perfect character joke — Selina's entire detachment from ordinary life condensed into not recognizing grass underfoot. The pilot's flat 'Grass, ma'am' is the ideal response. High character integration because this is Selina's essential condition: supremely powerful, completely insulated, totally helpless in basic reality.

jokes.text: Catherine: 'Are you sure you don't know where the hard drive of my film is?' while her mother stands on an airfield having just been removed from a malfunctioning presidential aircraft.
jokes.setup: Selina has just had to evacuate her plane. Her daughter's concern is not for her but for missing documentary footage.
jokes.punchline: Catherine's total indifference to her mother's humiliation — her only concern is her film — is the joke.
jokes.explanation: A functional character beat — Catherine's artistic self-absorption as a contrast to the human crisis. Works as a grace note in the sequence.

jokes.text: Selina walking back toward the White House while the inauguration parade passes — she appears to watch Montez's motorcade roll by.
jokes.setup: Her plane failed, she can't leave. The inauguration parade is happening.
jokes.punchline: The outgoing president is stranded at the airfield while her successor's parade passes her. She can see the celebration from which she's excluded.
jokes.explanation: The episode's central visual joke and one of its most powerful moments. The image — Selina stranded, watching the parade she should have been leading — is both devastating and hilarious. The comedy comes entirely from staging and character context. High originality because the physical situation is specific to this character's entire arc. The laugh comes from the recognition of total, irrevocable defeat made visual.

jokes.text: 'Maybe it'll ruin her parade.'
jokes.setup: Selina is watching Montez's inauguration parade, presumably from the airfield.
jokes.punchline: Her petty hope — that something will ruin Montez's parade — is her only source of comfort. It's the sentence of the defeated.
jokes.explanation: Five words that encapsulate Selina's character — her ambition reduced to petty spite. The line is both funny and genuinely sad. Economy is exceptional. A signature Veep moment.

jokes.text: Dan gets a job offer from a TV network, gets another offer from CBS News, and tells both people he's chosen CBS — 'Egan out.' Then immediately confirms CBS means Columbia Broadcasting.
jokes.setup: Dan is trying to parlay his White House TV work into a media career.
jokes.punchline: 'Egan out' as a self-important sign-off — then he immediately needs to confirm what CBS stands for, undermining the bravado completely.
jokes.explanation: 'Egan out' is a great Dan moment — pure performative swagger. The deflation of needing to confirm what CBS stands for is the right-sized puncture. Character-specific: Dan's confidence always slightly outpaces his knowledge.

jokes.text: Doctor to Jonah: 'You have a lump on your left testicle. I just want to biopsy it and we can take it from there.' / 'Take what from where?'
jokes.setup: Jonah is seeing a doctor following the basketball incident to his testicles.
jokes.punchline: The doctor says 'take it from there' — a common phrase. Jonah, already anxious about his testicle, interprets it literally and with maximum panic: 'Take what from where?'
jokes.explanation: A beautifully constructed wordplay/misdirection joke. 'Take it from there' is an idiom; in this context it terrifyingly sounds like removal. Jonah's panic-stricken literal interpretation is perfectly in character — his combination of arrogance and cowardice means this kind of threat collapses him instantly. The callback to the basketball injury makes the payoff richer.

jokes.text: Doctor: 'I'm married. I have the ring hiding under the gloves.' (responding to Jonah's inauguration ball invitation)
jokes.setup: Jonah has invited the doctor to the inauguration ball as a date in the middle of a testicular lump diagnosis.
jokes.punchline: The doctor was hiding her wedding ring under her medical gloves — she'd been tempted enough to conceal the ring, then thought better of it.
jokes.explanation: A funny detail — the ring-hiding under gloves — that adds texture to the Jonah/doctor dynamic. The implication that she was briefly considering it makes the joke richer.

jokes.text: 'Early detection would have prevented this. We really encourage regular self-exams.' / 'Yeah, I know.'
jokes.setup: Doctor gives the standard self-exam speech after finding the lump.
jokes.punchline: Jonah's 'Yeah, I know' implies he's been aware of the lump and just... not done anything about it. His cavalier attitude toward his own body is the joke.
jokes.explanation: A small, dark character beat. Jonah's 'Yeah, I know' is the funniest possible response — he's not surprised, he just didn't care. The dark health implication adds Veep-appropriate grimness.

jokes.text: 'Selina, good news. We found the missing Nevada ballots. You won.'
jokes.setup: Selina has just lived through the worst day of her political life — stranded at an airfield, watching Montez's parade.
jokes.punchline: The election she lost was actually hers to win. She has already vacated the office. It's too late. She won and lost and it doesn't matter.
jokes.explanation: The series finale joke. The entire episode — the series, really — is punctuated by this single devastating revelation. The comedy is in the structure: everything that happened was avoidable, and the discovery comes one minute too late. The economy is perfect: the information is delivered in six words, and then the episode ends. This is the show's thesis on power, luck, and futility delivered as a single line. It's not just funny — it's the joke that makes every other joke in the episode retroactively funnier. Justifiably the episode's highest-scored joke.

tmdb_overview: Selina and her staff prepare for inauguration day. Mike suffers exhaustion. Catherine gets a makeover.
insight: Inauguration day descends into chaos: 56 jokes in 101 minutes of pure cringe.
